title: 10 Million Dark: Cuba's Power Grid Collapses Again Amid US Embargo
description: More than 10 million people in Cuba face total blackout as the national electrical grid collapses for the second time in a week due to chronic fuel shortages and a US blockade.
lede: More than 10 million Cubans have been plunged into darkness after the nation's electrical grid collapsed for the second time in a week. The total failure underscores the severity of an ongoing energy crisis driven by a US fuel blockade and rapidly deteriorating infrastructure.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: Cuba's energy ministry confirmed a total disconnection of the National Electrical System, leaving more than 10 million residents and businesses without power. This marks the third major blackout to strike the Caribbean nation this month.

Grid operator UNE stated it is gradually restoring electricity, prioritizing vital facilities such as hospitals and water systems. By Sunday afternoon, power had been returned to roughly half of Havana, according to the city's electricity company.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The blackouts have sparked rare public dissent in a country where unauthorized demonstrations are illegal. Frustrated locals took to the streets in central Havana on Monday, banging pots and pans, while protesters in the central town of Morón reportedly set fire to the Communist Party headquarters.
The crisis is directly tied to severe fuel shortages exacerbated by a US-imposed blockade, cutting off essential foreign oil imports required to keep the aging power stations operational.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: International socialist organizations arrived in Havana over the weekend to deliver aid, including solar panels, food, and medicine. The "Nuestra America" convoy, a flotilla of supplies leaving Mexico, is also expected to dock in Havana's port on Monday following delays from rough seas.

The geopolitical tension surrounding the crisis is escalating. Former US President Donald Trump has suggested a "friendly takeover" of Cuba, demanding the removal of Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel as a prerequisite for lifting the fuel embargo.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: The American and Cuban governments have initiated bilateral talks aimed at resolving the crisis, though progress remains unclear. Cuban Deputy Foreign Minister Carlos Fernandez de Cossio firmly stated that the country's political system and leadership are not open for negotiation.
In response to US rhetoric, President Díaz-Canel announced the island has activated a preparation plan to raise readiness for defense against potential military aggression.
